titleStmt.title: A Transcription of John in Codex Corbeiensis
VL8
The New Testament
The Gospel according to John
john
titleStmt.funder: The Arts and Humanities Research Council

sponsor.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing

publisher.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing
University of Birmingham

availability.p: Licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0): available for re-use provided attribution is made to the original creators (the IGNTP and any named participants) and an indication is given of any changes made. See further .

msItem.title: Quattuor Euangelia
Four Gospels in order Mt Jo Lc Mc

surrogates.bibl: Black and white photographs in ITSEE, supplied by Vetus Latina-Institut.
Black and white digitized microfilm on CD in ITSEE supplied by BNF.

listBibl.bibl: E.S. Buchanan (ed.) The Four Gospels from the Codex Corbeiensis (Oxford Old Latin Biblical Texts 5). Oxford 1907.

projectDesc.p: This transcription was made by the Verbum Project as part of an online and printed edition of the Old Latin versions of John between 2003 and 2010.

editorialDecl.p: This transcription covers ff. 40v to 87r. The manuscript is lacunose in John 17:16-18:9 and 20:23-21:8. It includes capitula preceding the Gospel (Type I). The manuscript has been heavily corrected by more than one later hand. One corrector has replaced some final superlines with the letter 'n' (but reinforced those for 'm', e.g. John 3:26). Sometimes a superline stands for 'nt' (e.g. 12:2, 19:40, 21:8). The first hand also displays some confusion between 'm' and 'nt' (e.g. 15:7, 15:21), and has a habit of omitting single letters (e.g. 't' in 15:8) and other unusual orthography (e.g. 'misitset' in 13:2 and 'intinxitset' in 13:26).The transcription includes foliation, lineation, all punctuation and abbreviations. digraphs and digraphs are included, as is a corrector's use of the 7-shaped 'et'. Corrections to punctuation have not been recorded. The initial transcription file was a plain text file for use with COLLATE. Further information about the procedures followed may be found in the electronic edition at
This second edition of the file was converted to XML by Catherine Smith using the project's Python conversion scripts 1.0.
The XML specifications used are version 1.5 of the IGNTP Guidelines available at

revisionDesc.change: Adjustment of transcription to version 1.5 of the XML by Hugh Houghton.
Reconversion of the original plain text file using Python scripts to version 1.4 of the XML by Catherine Smith and manual checking.
Hyphen entity replaced by lb attribute break="no" by Hugh Houghton
Changes made in order to display properly with VLI.xsl transformation by Hugh Houghton
Correction to John 4:38 and 4:39, and change to verse division in 5:18-19 by Hugh Houghton
Transcription converted to the TEI XML schema and corrected for parsing by Hugh Houghton

ab.note: The Gospel according to Matthew finishes in this column.
On this page, the first line of each capitulum is in darker ink, but it has been recorded as rubrics in keeping with the subsequent pages. The first three letters of 'Iohannes' may be in gold.
For this spelling, compare also the running title of folio 55.
A decorative line of hederae; the rest of the page is blank.
The 'n' is possibly deleted by overdot - except that this is not found elsewhere inthe MS.
correction possibly work of Buchanans eighteenth-century corrector ffnou
The corrector adds the 'i' at the end of the previous line and deletes it from the beginning of the next line.
us apparently reinked in a later hand
There are ink marks after 'uocatis', but they seem most likely to be ink from the addition of 'tunc' on the next page.
The word 'Uirtutis' is written by a later hand in the top right hand corner of the page.
The last 3 letters of perhiberet are written as a ligature and may have been added by a later hand.
It is not clear whether this line is rubricated.
nasal bar possibly later addition the first hand tends to reserve them for the ends of lines, and to offset them to the right
A corrector, possibly the first hand, has changed the word-division from ca-rne to car-ne.
Not clear whether this line is rubricated.
The rest of this column has been overwritten by a later hand, because the writing is so faint.
The overwriter appears to have misread the initial 'c' as '&'.
Quire signature in bottom right corner.
final m possibly a later addition
photograph very faint text reconstructed largely with Buchanans help
The point at which the marginal correction should be introduced is unclear, as is the erasure of 'ihs', but it seems likely the corrector intended the Vulgate reading.
same hand as in previous verse
in margin to right of line 10: ffff, probably in hand ffd
both additions in this verse marginal probably ffd

This column has numerous marginal lines added by a later hand against verses 54, 55, and the second half of 56. In the last case, the lines around the text appear to indicate deletion: it is not clear whether the indication was to delete or emphasise verse 54-55, where the text is not so obviously circled. (It could be that a text used for comparison was missing 54-55 through homoeoarcton, and so deletion was intended.).
The underlining of the second half of verse 56 has been interpreted as a deletion. See further the note at the top of the column.
Possible correction to 'dixisset', but too faint to be certain.
The corrector has deleted the initial 's' of 'scrutare'.
It is possible that the first letter of 'de' has been erased by an underdot, but erasures in this manuscript are normally indicated by lines.
The corrector's intention was presumably to read 'superunxit', but the 'n' rather than the 's' is deleted.
Quire signature XI in bottom right hand margin.

This verse has been added in the central margin by a later hand. The signe de renvoi actually appears over 'haec' at the end of the next line, but coding constraints mean that it has been inserted in the Vulgate sequence of verses.
The corrector appears to have misread 'est et' as 'esset', and therefore added a further 'et'.
In addition to this correction, a 'r' has been written above the original 'o', presumably before the corrector decided to use the p(ro) abbreviation.
The small final 'm' may be first hand.
Quire signature XII in bottom right margin.
The 'r' on the end of 'maior' may have been added by a corrector.
The 're' in 'credatis' may have been added by a corrector.
'me' may have been added by a corrector.
The 's' of 'petrus' may be a correction.
The corrector may have misread the 't' as an 'i'.
The corrector has not fully deleted the final 'uos', despite supplying the word in full in the margin.
The 'n' of 'mundo' is probably added by a later hand in place of a nasal bar.
Transposition marked by inverted triangles of dots.

A corrector has added three cola, so as to read " : uobis : et : ". It is not clear whether these are deletion or transposition marks, and what the intended reading was: the Vulgate has been preferred.
'in' may have been added by a later hand.
There is a note in a later hand which reads something like: 'uersus desunt ad v ·9· capitis 18'.
Quire signature XIII in bottom margin.
The manuscript resumes in John 18:9; the absent leaves were missing before the pages were numbered.
There is no deletion mark on 'scriptura', but 'sermo' is written over the top of it.
Some of the text between here and the end of the column has been re-inked by a corrector.
Modern note in bottom right hand corner to the effect that one folio is missing which would have contained John 20:23 to 21:8.
The manuscript resumes in John 21:8; the absent leaves were missing before the pages were numbered.
Addition in central margin, upside-down, apparently reading 'sefeyi'.
The explicit is interspersed with decorative lines.
The explicit is followed by the introduction and capitula of Luke.
